EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure area tracked from Western Illinois into Southern Wisconsin. Numerous thunderstorms developed with most producing small hail, however one large hail report was received. In addition, lightning struck a tree and the buried gas line below it. The gas line ruptured causing a fire in the front yard of the residence.

A deepening low pressure area tracked from Western Illinois into Southern Wisconsin. Numerous thunderstorms developed with most producing small hail, however one large hail report was received. In addition, lightning struck a tree and the buried gas line below it. The gas line ruptured causing a fire in the front yard of the residence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree and then traveled into the ground rupturing a gas line. This sparked a fire in the front yard of the residence. Two homes were evacuated for a short time. No homes were damaged and no one was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western Wisconsin experienced strong winds in early April after a cold front moved across the region. Gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common, although a peak gust of 53 mph occurred in La Crosse.  A camper was tipped over by the strong winds on U.S. Highway 18 near Patch Grove (Grant County) and was destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph were common across Buffalo County during the morning and early afternoon of April 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Tuesday, July 5th, two areas of thunderstorms that developed across west central Minnesota, moved southeast across central and southern Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin.  ||The southern storm affected the southern part of the Twin Cities metro area during the late afternoon, and moved eastward into west central Wisconsin from near Prescott and Hager City to Eau Claire Wisconsin by early evening. These storms produced damaging winds and two tornadoes near Plum City and Porcupine.||The hardest hit areas were in Pierce County from Prescott and Bay City, eastward to Ellsworth, Salem and Maiden Rock. Hundreds of trees were blown down with some structural damage to bridges near Ellsworth and home damage in Plum City. Local law enforcement officers reported a swept away trailer house at Highway 10 and 490th Avenue in the Town of Salem. There was also damage to a milking parlor in Ellsworth where 3 feet of water filled the calf barn. ||The Pepin County Emergency Manager released a press statement that provided detailed information on widespread damage throughout the county. There was also structural damage to homes and agricultural, and at least three commercial properties in the Town of Waubeek's business park were damaged. ||In addition to structural damage to homes and businesses, crop damage was extensive and the local emergency management submitted a disaster report based|on the widespread nature of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed from a storm that would go on to produce a tornado at Nugget Lake (see separate entry for the tornado). Local law enforcement officials also indicated a trailer house was blown over at Highway 10 and 490th Avenue in the Town of Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure and the associated surge of relatively milder and moist air brought a few inches of snow to portions of South Central WI. Some vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow and gusty southeast winds up to 25 mph created snow covered roads and hazardous travel.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and tree branches down from La Valle to Rock Springs to Baraboo.

Thunderstorms, that moved along a frontal boundary, produced heavy rainfall across northern Wisconsin on the evening of October 17th and isolated large hail after midnight. The heaviest rain fell in a swath from far northern Lincoln County to Florence County. Some of the heaviest rain fell in Rhinelander (Oneida County) where there was minor street flooding. The rainfall total of 4.64 inches at Rhinelander, most of which fell in just a few hours, set a new calendar day rainfall record for October. It also tied for second wettest calendar day on record at Rhinelander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail fell as a thunderstorm passed through Newald.

It also tied for second wettest calendar day on record at Rhinelander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused street flooding in Rhinelander, mainly west of the Wisconsin River in the area of Davenport Street and Maple Street.

A man who was missing since Jan. 5th was found dead inside a carport at his neighbor's house in West Allis. The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's Office determined the cause of death as hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's Office diagnosed hypothermia as the cause of death of a missing man who was found dead inside his neighbor's carport that had a zippered cover. The man was only wearing a shirt, sweat pants, and socks. Temperatures were in the 20s and 30s Fahrenheit from the time he was missing, which was Jan. 5th until Jan. 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's Office diagnosed the death of a woman as hypothermia. Temperatures were below zero Fahrenheit with wind chill temperatures 20 below zero at the time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's Office diagnosed the death of a woman as hypothermia. Temperatures were below zero Fahrenheit with wind chill temperatures 20 below zero at the time. Video from a business recorded her collapsing in front of a home around 4:25 a.m. She was not dressed properly for the bitter cold and was found by a passerby around 9:00 a.m.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Kenosha County Medical Examiner reported a death due to hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kenosha County Medical Examiner reported the cause of death of a woman as hypothermia due to environmental cold exposure. The woman was found by a postal worker at 11:30 am. The low temperature earlier that morning in Kenosha was 4 below zero Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved across Northern Wisconsin and swept an occluded front across Southern WI in the late morning and afternoon hours. Strong westerly wind gusts of 58 to 65 mph occurred over Southeast WI and far Southern WI.  Four Semi-trucks were blown over blocking all or partial lanes on the Interstates. Scattered downed whole trees and branches were reported with some falling on power lines, homes, or parked vehicles. Power outages reached a maximum of 26,000 customers. Scattered reports of shingles and aluminum fascia torn from homes and garages were received. Other reports of minor structural damage including downed signs were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered downed trees and branches were reported with some falling onto power lines, homes, or parked vehicles. Scattered reports of shingles, siding, and aluminum fascia torn from homes and garages were received. Other reports of minor structural damage included dislodged or downed signs, and scaffolding blown into traffic in downtown Waukesha. A traffic light was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved southward across the Upper Midwest during the weekend of January 16th and 17th. Actual air temperatures fell into the teens, and to near 20 degrees below zero Sunday morning. As wind speeds averaged between 5 to 10 mph, this created wind chill values as low as -35F for several hours during the morning of Sunday, January 17th.  ||Although wind speeds averaged lower in west central Wisconsin due to terrain and vegetation, low temperatures were colder than in Minnesota, and thus a few wind chill values dropped to -40F after sunrise. This was reported at Cumberland which had their coldest wind chill values prior to 9 AM LST at -40-F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather observations indicated that wind chill values dropped to around -35F for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow across northwest Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of January 27th transitioned to lake effect snow overnight into the morning of January 28th. A couple locations in northern Iron County reported more than 8 of snow within 24 hours, including 9.3 in Gile, WI and 10 in Hurley, WI. Ironwood, MI, which is just across the border from Iron County, reported about 11.5.
Arctic air spread into western and central Wisconsin on the morning of January 17th, producing wind chills of 35 to 40 below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped into the -35 to -40 F range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man found lying in his front yard at daybreak had died from hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jefferson County Medical Examiner reported a death of man due to hypothermia. The man was found lying in his front yard at daybreak. It is unknown how many hours the man was lying in the snow. Temperatures were in the 20s Fahrenheit during the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area brought a couple rounds of snow to Southern WI during the evening of February 29th through the afternoon of March 1st. Three to six inches of snow including some blowing and drifting snow occurred. Slide-offs and accidents were common throughout the region. Brief near-blizzard conditions were reported from the late morning into the early afternoon on March 1st when the heaviest snow affected the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved across Northern Wisconsin and swept an occluded front across Southern WI in the late morning and afternoon hours. Strong westerly wind gusts of 58 to 65 mph occurred over Southeast WI and far Southern WI.  Four Semi-trucks were blown over blocking all or partial lanes on the Interstates. Scattered downed whole trees and branches were reported with some falling on power lines, homes, or parked vehicles. Power outages reached a maximum of 26,000 customers. Scattered reports of shingles and aluminum fascia torn from homes and garages were received. Other reports of minor structural damage including downed signs were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The majority of the roof of an assisted living home in Genoa City was torn off. The 33 residents were unhurt but relocated. Scattered reports of shingles, siding, and aluminum fascia torn from homes and garages were received. Scattered downed trees and branches were reported with some falling onto power lines, homes, or parked vehicles.  A transformer was damaged. A semi-truck was blown over by the strong westerly winds on I-94.  The interstate was closed for some time for removal and clean-up. The Kenosha Airport measured a maximum wind gust of 63 mph for the afternoon and a maximum sustained wind of 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 43-year-old woman, last seen late on the night of January 10th, was found dead outside of a Green Bay tavern on the morning of January 11th.  Temperatures during the time for which she was unaccounted were in the 2 below to 6 below zero range with wind chills mainly ranging from 20 below to 23 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills at Green Bay were generally in the 20 below to 23 below zero range from late on the night of January 10th through early morning January 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hypothermia was a significant factor in the death of a woman who went missing for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A missing rural Portage woman was found dead near a frozen stream. She had been missing since Jan. 28th. The Columbia County Medical Examiner's office reported Hypothermia was a major factor in her death with alcohol a contributing factor. Low temperatures the morning of Jan. 29th were in the single digits Fahrenheit. Otherwise temperatures ranged from the 20s to the 40s during the remainder of time she was missing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across Iowa and southern Wisconsin. After the low moved east of the area the snow transitioned to lake effect snow. ||There was a band of about 6 to 10 inches of the low-induced snow that fell from the Twin Cities to the Rice Lake, Hayward, and Hurley areas.  Additional lake enhancement and post-storm lake effect snow showers resulted in total snowfall in excess of one foot across northern Iron County.  Specific snowfall totals across northwest Wisconsin included 16.6 in Gile and 11.0 near Saxon (both in Iron County), 12.0 near Butternut and 8.7 in Ashland (both in Ashland County), 10.0 several miles south of Butternut and 8.0 in Park Falls (both in Price County), 9.0 near Sarona and 8.0 in Springbrook (both in Washburn County), 8.0 in Stone Lake (Sawyer County), and 7.0 in Siren (Burnett County).

Scattered thunderstorms developed during the early evening of March 6th across central Wisconsin. Some of these storms became strong enough to produce large hail across Juneau County. The largest hail reported was half dollar sized in Necedah.

Scattered thunderstorms developed during the early evening of March 6th across central Wisconsin. Some of these storms became strong enough to produce large hail across Juneau County. The largest hail reported was half dollar sized in Necedah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail fell in Necedah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the early evening of March 6th across central Wisconsin. Some of these storms became strong enough to produce large hail across Juneau County. The largest hail reported was half dollar sized in Necedah.

Parts of central Wisconsin were covered with 4 to 6 inches of snow over the first part of March. As this snow melted, the runoff increased flows on rivers and streams and produced some minor flooding along the Yellow River in Juneau County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melting snow pushed the Yellow River out of its banks in Necedah. The river crested at 15.7 feet, almost three-quarters of a foot above the flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked from Central WI to the eastern portion of Upper MI. Strong westerly wind gusts occurred as the low moved to the northeast. Many tree limbs or whole trees were brought down with some snapped or uprooted. Some fell on electrical wires resulting in power outages. We Energies reported 21,000 customers without power at one point, while Madison Gas and Electric reported 2,200 customers without power in the Madison area around 4 pm CDT. A few utility poles were snapped across Southern WI, while one semi-truck was overturned in Iowa Co. A large road sign was blown down on I-94 at US highway 45. Multiple lanes of I-94 were closed to remove the sign. Some shingle damage and roof damage occurred due to the high winds or trees falling on the roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted most of the area during the afternoon and evening hours of February 2nd as a strong low pressure system moved from the central Plains to northern Lower Michigan, then continued northeast to western Quebec.||Five to eight inches of snow fell in about 6 hours during the afternoon of the 2nd.  Blustery east and northeast winds gusted to around 35 mph as the storm system passed to the south.  The wet, heavy snow was accompanied by lightning and thunder in Manitowoc County and Calumet County.  Some freezing rain and mixed precipitation also fell across parts of central and east central Wisconsin.  The highest snowfall totals from the storm included 7.6 inches southwest of Big Falls (Waupaca Co.), 7.5 inches in the Wautoma area (Waushara Co.) and near Suring (Oconto Co.), 7.2 inches near Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.) and near Pulcifer (Shawano Co.), and 7.0 inches near Plover (Portage Co.).||An additional 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the Fox Valley and lakeshore counties on the 3rd as an upper level disturbance moved across the state.||Snow moved into Vilas County on the afternoon of the 2nd, and continued through the morning of the 3rd due to lake effect snow showers behind the departing surface low pressure system. Highest snowfall totals in Vilas County included 7.8 inches at Lac du Flambeau, and 7.0 inches at Rest Lake and near Manitowish Waters.||New daily snowfall records were set for February 2nd at Appleton (Outagamie Co.), Stevens Point (Portage Co.), and Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.), each breaking their previous record by at least an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals included 7.8 inches at Lac du Flambeau, and 7.0 inches at Rest Lake and near Manitowish Waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over portions of northern and central Wisconsin on March 15th caused some river flooding to occur. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 2.07 inches of rain fell near Neillsville.

Specific snowfall totals across northwest Wisconsin included 16.6 in Gile and 11.0 near Saxon (both in Iron County), 12.0 near Butternut and 8.7 in Ashland (both in Ashland County), 10.0 several miles south of Butternut and 8.0 in Park Falls (both in Price County), 9.0 near Sarona and 8.0 in Springbrook (both in Washburn County), 8.0 in Stone Lake (Sawyer County), and 7.0 in Siren (Burnett County).

Strong low pressure tracked from Central WI to the eastern portion of Upper MI. Strong westerly wind gusts occurred as the low moved to the northeast. Many tree limbs or whole trees were brought down with some snapped or uprooted. Some fell on electrical wires resulting in power outages. We Energies reported 21,000 customers without power at one point, while Madison Gas and Electric reported 2,200 customers without power in the Madison area around 4 pm CDT. A few utility poles were snapped across Southern WI, while one semi-truck was overturned in Iowa Co. A large road sign was blown down on I-94 at US highway 45. Multiple lanes of I-94 were closed to remove the sign. Some shingle damage and roof damage occurred due to the high winds or trees falling on the roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered downed trees and limbs across the county with some falling onto power lines.|WE Energies reported 2,500 customers without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the Upper Midwest with snowfall in excess of a foot near the Twin Cities Metropolitan Area, to between 9 and 12 inches northeast across portions of west central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, February 2nd. ||This storm system began to organize in the central Rockies Monday, February, 1st with heavy snowfall along the lee of the Rockies, and out into the Central Plains by Monday afternoon. This area of snow continued to move east-northeast across the plains, and into the Upper Midwest by Tuesday morning, February 2nd. Snow started to fall heavily along the Iowa, Minnesota border Tuesday morning before 9 AM CST, and then moved northward, and affected the southern half of the Twin Cities Metropolitan Area by noon. This snow spread across west central Wisconsin during the afternoon, with a persistent band of higher snowfall rates near Hudson, northeast to Rice Lake for several hours.  ||Due to a more northwest shift of this system as it moved out into the plains, and Upper Midwest, drier air was able to move into parts of west central Wisconsin, which kept snowfall totals in this area lower than expected. This area also received some freezing drizzle and sleet as warmer air aloft managed to move farther to the north. ||As this system moved from southwest Iowa Tuesday evening, to southern Wisconsin by Wednesday morning, February 3rd, snowfall began to taper off after midnight. Lingering light snow ended in west central Wisconsin by noon. ||Total snowfall across the area by Wednesday morning ranged from... ||9 to 12 inches from Hudson, Roberts, Clear Lake, Barron and Rice Lake, to 5 to 7 inches from Durand to Eau Claire, Chippewa Falls, and Boyd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources from local spotters, and officials indicated that heavy snow fell across the county. Locally, 6 inches of snow fell in the county. In addition, sleet, freezing drizzle and light snow also occurred through the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted most of the area during the afternoon and evening hours of February 2nd as a strong low pressure system moved from the central Plains to northern Lower Michigan, then continued northeast to western Quebec.||Five to eight inches of snow fell in about 6 hours during the afternoon of the 2nd.  Blustery east and northeast winds gusted to around 35 mph as the storm system passed to the south.  The wet, heavy snow was accompanied by lightning and thunder in Manitowoc County and Calumet County.  Some freezing rain and mixed precipitation also fell across parts of central and east central Wisconsin.  The highest snowfall totals from the storm included 7.6 inches southwest of Big Falls (Waupaca Co.), 7.5 inches in the Wautoma area (Waushara Co.) and near Suring (Oconto Co.), 7.2 inches near Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.) and near Pulcifer (Shawano Co.), and 7.0 inches near Plover (Portage Co.).||An additional 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the Fox Valley and lakeshore counties on the 3rd as an upper level disturbance moved across the state.||Snow moved into Vilas County on the afternoon of the 2nd, and continued through the morning of the 3rd due to lake effect snow showers behind the departing surface low pressure system. Highest snowfall totals in Vilas County included 7.8 inches at Lac du Flambeau, and 7.0 inches at Rest Lake and near Manitowish Waters.||New daily snowfall records were set for February 2nd at Appleton (Outagamie Co.), Stevens Point (Portage Co.), and Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.), each breaking their previous record by at least an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest storm total snowfall in Shawano County was 7.2 inches near Pulcifer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of mild, moist, and unstable air aloft brought scattered thunderstorms to Southern WI. Two homes suffered significant structural damage from fires after being struck by lightning. Small hail was reported throughout the evening with the largest hail stone the size of a nickel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home causing a significant fire. The Sheriff's office said the house suffered significant roof, smoke, and water damage. An adult, a child, and a dog escaped unharmed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western and central Wisconsin was covered by 5 to 11 inches of snow on February 2nd. This snow fell as a winter storm tracked from Kansas into lower Michigan. A band of heavy snow developed ahead of the storm, which moved into western Wisconsin during the morning of the 2nd and produced snowfall rates up to two inches an hour. This caused roads to quickly become snow covered. The highest reported snowfall amount was 11.2 inches near Cashton (Monroe County). Blowing and drifting snow became an issue as the storm moved past with wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph. Most schools closed February 2nd in advance of the storm and many of these remained closed on the 3rd as well. In addition to the school closings, the shopping mall and colleges in the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) also closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 7 to 9 inches of snow across Adams County. The highest reported total was 9.5 inches in Friendship. Considerable blowing and drifting snow occurred with wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph.

A woman passed away from exposure in early February in the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County). The woman left a drinking establishment early February 8th and her body was found the next day. Observations from the automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport indicated temperatures were between 15 and 20 degrees, light snow was falling, sustained wind speeds were between 15 and 25 mph creating wind chills of 0 to 5 above while the woman was outdoors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman that left a La Crosse drinking establishment during the early morning hours of February 8th passed away from exposure. Observations from the automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport indicated temperatures were between 15 and 20 degrees, light snow was falling, sustained wind speeds were between 15 and 25 mph creating wind chills of 0 to 5 above while the woman was outdoors.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph occurred across portions of Crawford County during the late morning and early afternoon of February 19th. A private weather station in Mount Sterling recorded a 54 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area brought a couple rounds of snow to Southern WI from the evening of February 29th through the afternoon of March 1st. Three to six inches of snow including some blowing and drifting snow occurred. Slide-offs and accidents were common throughout the region. Brief near-blizzard conditions were reported from the late morning into the early afternoon on March 1st when the heaviest snow affected the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wisconsin River near Portage reached major flood stage after 2.00-2.50 inches of rain fell in the Upper Wisconsin River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wisconsin River near Portage reached major flood stage, eventually cresting at 19.62 feet. Widespread flooding of the Blackhawk Park and Long Lake areas occurred  with many flooded roads and road closures in that area.  Some seasonal homes along Old River Road were surrounded by floodwaters. Levee Rd., west of Portage but south of the river, was sandbagged in order to keep open. Farther south in the Town of Dekorra, a few homes had floodwaters in the first floor along with the flooding of outbuildings.  Floodwaters also flowed over County Highway J, V, and River Oaks Road in the Town of Dekorra. Some residents had to use canoes or boats for transportation to and from their homes. On the west side of Portage and north of the river, floodwaters affected Summit Ave. and River Shores Rd.

Winds of 45 to 60 mph battered portions of western Wisconsin on March 16th. A strong area of low pressure moved across the region with the strong winds occurring in the tight pressure gradient both on the front and back sides of the low. The highest reported wind gust was 61 mph from a private weather station in Mt. Sterling (Crawford County). These strong winds blew trees down and onto power lines in the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport and a mesonet reporting station near St. Joseph recorded wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph on March 16th. The highest recorded wind gust was 49 mph at the La Crosse airport and 45 mph near St. Joseph. These strong winds blew trees down and onto power lines in La Crosse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western Wisconsin received both ice and snow from a late winter storm that hit the area on March 23rd and 24th. Ice accumulations between a quarter and a half inch occurred mainly across Crawford and Grant Counties. Up to a half inch of ice accumulation was reported in Eastman (Crawford County). North of the ice accumulation, between 4 and 13 inches of snow fell. North winds of 20 to 30 mph created blowing and drifting snow for several hours on March 24th. The highest reported snow total was 13 inches near Riplinger (Clark County) and in Osseo (Trempealeau County). The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for this storm. All the colleges and universities in the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) canceled classes for the evening of the 23rd through the morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 2 to 6 inches of snow across Vernon County. The snow was mixed with sleet and freezing rain at times during the afternoon and evening of the 23rd. The highest reported snow amount was 6 inches in De Soto. North winds of 20 to 30 mph created several hours of blowing and drifting snow on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the Upper Midwest with snowfall in excess of a foot near the Twin Cities Metropolitan Area, to between 9 and 12 inches northeast across portions of west central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, February 2nd. ||This storm system began to organize in the central Rockies Monday, February, 1st with heavy snowfall along the lee of the Rockies, and out into the Central Plains by Monday afternoon. This area of snow continued to move east-northeast across the plains, and into the Upper Midwest by Tuesday morning, February 2nd. Snow started to fall heavily along the Iowa, Minnesota border Tuesday morning before 9 AM CST, and then moved northward, and affected the southern half of the Twin Cities Metropolitan Area by noon. This snow spread across west central Wisconsin during the afternoon, with a persistent band of higher snowfall rates near Hudson, northeast to Rice Lake for several hours.  ||Due to a more northwest shift of this system as it moved out into the plains, and Upper Midwest, drier air was able to move into parts of west central Wisconsin, which kept snowfall totals in this area lower than expected. This area also received some freezing drizzle and sleet as warmer air aloft managed to move farther to the north. ||As this system moved from southwest Iowa Tuesday evening, to southern Wisconsin by Wednesday morning, February 3rd, snowfall began to taper off after midnight. Lingering light snow ended in west central Wisconsin by noon. ||Total snowfall across the area by Wednesday morning ranged from... ||9 to 12 inches from Hudson, Roberts, Clear Lake, Barron and Rice Lake, to 5 to 7 inches from Durand to Eau Claire, Chippewa Falls, and Boyd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources from local spotters, and officials indicated that heavy snow fell across the county. Locally, 6 inches of snow fell across the county.  A mixture of sleet and freezing drizzle also occurred during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western and central Wisconsin was covered by 5 to 11 inches of snow on February 2nd. This snow fell as a winter storm tracked from Kansas into lower Michigan. A band of heavy snow developed ahead of the storm, which moved into western Wisconsin during the morning of the 2nd and produced snowfall rates up to two inches an hour. This caused roads to quickly become snow covered. The highest reported snowfall amount was 11.2 inches near Cashton (Monroe County). Blowing and drifting snow became an issue as the storm moved past with wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph. Most schools closed February 2nd in advance of the storm and many of these remained closed on the 3rd as well. In addition to the school closings, the shopping mall and colleges in the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) also closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 6 to 8 inches of snow across Trempealeau County. The highest reported total was 8 inches near Independence and the city of Trempealeau. Considerable blowing and drifting snow occurred with wind gusts of 25 to 30 mph. The highest reported wind gust was 29 mph by a mesonet reporting station south of Arcadia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 6 to 11 inches of snow across Vernon County. The highest reported total was 11 inches near Viroqua and Valley. Considerable blowing and drifting snow occurred with wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph. The highest reported wind gust was 33 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Viroqua airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted most of the area during the afternoon and evening hours of February 2nd as a strong low pressure system moved from the central Plains to northern Lower Michigan, then continued northeast to western Quebec.||Five to eight inches of snow fell in about 6 hours during the afternoon of the 2nd.  Blustery east and northeast winds gusted to around 35 mph as the storm system passed to the south.  The wet, heavy snow was accompanied by lightning and thunder in Manitowoc County and Calumet County.  Some freezing rain and mixed precipitation also fell across parts of central and east central Wisconsin.  The highest snowfall totals from the storm included 7.6 inches southwest of Big Falls (Waupaca Co.), 7.5 inches in the Wautoma area (Waushara Co.) and near Suring (Oconto Co.), 7.2 inches near Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.) and near Pulcifer (Shawano Co.), and 7.0 inches near Plover (Portage Co.).||An additional 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the Fox Valley and lakeshore counties on the 3rd as an upper level disturbance moved across the state.||Snow moved into Vilas County on the afternoon of the 2nd, and continued through the morning of the 3rd due to lake effect snow showers behind the departing surface low pressure system. Highest snowfall totals in Vilas County included 7.8 inches at Lac du Flambeau, and 7.0 inches at Rest Lake and near Manitowish Waters.||New daily snowfall records were set for February 2nd at Appleton (Outagamie Co.), Stevens Point (Portage Co.), and Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.), each breaking their previous record by at least an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest storm total snowfall in Waushara County was 7.5 inches in the Wautoma area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area brought a couple rounds of snow to Southern WI from the evening of February 29th through the afternoon of March 1st. Three to six inches of snow including some blowing and drifting snow occurred. Slide-offs and accidents were common throughout the region. Brief near-blizzard conditions were reported from the late morning into the early afternoon on March 1st when the heaviest snow affected the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked from the Central Great Plains through Southeast Wisconsin bringing several inches of snow to Sauk, Columbia, Marquette, and Green Lake Counties. Gusty east winds to 30 mph prevailed during the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area brought a couple rounds of snow to Southern WI from the evening of February 29th through the afternoon of March 1st. Three to six inches of snow including some blowing and drifting snow occurred. Slide-offs and accidents were common throughout the region. Brief near-blizzard conditions were reported from the late morning into the early afternoon on March 1st when the heaviest snow affected the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western Wisconsin received both ice and snow from a late winter storm that hit the area on March 23rd and 24th. Ice accumulations between a quarter and a half inch occurred mainly across Crawford and Grant Counties. Up to a half inch of ice accumulation was reported in Eastman (Crawford County). North of the ice accumulation, between 4 and 13 inches of snow fell. North winds of 20 to 30 mph created blowing and drifting snow for several hours on March 24th. The highest reported snow total was 13 inches near Riplinger (Clark County) and in Osseo (Trempealeau County). The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for this storm. All the colleges and universities in the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) canceled classes for the evening of the 23rd through the morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported around 4 inches of snow across Juneau County. The snow was mixed with sleet at times across the southern portion of the county during the evening of the 23rd into the morning of the 24th. North winds of 20 to 30 mph created several hours of blowing and drifting snow on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds of 45 to 60 mph battered portions of western Wisconsin on March 16th. A strong area of low pressure moved across the region with the strong winds occurring in the tight pressure gradient both on the front and back sides of the low. The highest reported wind gust was 61 mph from a private weather station in Mt. Sterling (Crawford County). These strong winds blew trees down and onto power lines in the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet reporting equipment near Arcadia reported wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph on March 16th. The highest recorded wind gust was 53 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bursts of accumulating snow occurred as intensifying low pressure moved across Southern WI and into Lower MI. There was an initial burst of snow in the morning followed by numerous heavy snow showers through the day. The snow and wind gusts of 35-50 mph made for snow covered roads and brief blizzard conditions. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snow showers producing brief whiteout conditions and snow covered roads. Northwesterly wind gusts of 35-45 mph. Snow accumulation of 1-3 inches. Many accidents on bridges and overpasses. Several accidents near Miller Park. I-43 northbound was closed for a time at Capitol Drive due to a cluster of accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system produced a swath of 1.5 to 3 inches of rain over much of central Wisconsin from the evening of March 15th into the morning of the 16th.  |Runoff from the heavy rain caused flooding of, mainly low-lying, areas along most of the main rivers across central Wisconsin.  The heavy rain caused ponding of water roads, resulting in the closure of roads in the Merrill area (Lincoln Co.).||Some of the heaviest rainfall totals included:  3.30 inches near New Rome (Wood Co.); 3.00 inches near Port Edwards (Wood Co.); 2.95 inches near Ogdensburg (Waupaca Co.); 2.86 inches near Waupaca (Waupaca Co.), at Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.), and near Evergreen (Marathon Co.); and 2.85 inches near Wautoma (Waushara Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning is believed to be the cause of a fire that caused substantial damage to a home near Sturgeon Bay.  The damage total is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed north of a warm front during the afternoon of June 14th. As these storms moved across southwest Wisconsin, they produced heavy rain that caused some flash flooding to occur in and near Prairie du Chien (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding to occur in Prairie du Chien and along State Highway 27 where it comes down off the ridge and into Prairie du Chien.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fatality occurred in a two-vehicle crash in Fond du Lac County. Snow showers and a light coating of snow was on the roads at the time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One fatality occurred in a two-vehicle crash on State Highway 23 east of Fond du Lac. Snow showers were occurring at the time with a light coating of snow on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed across Southeast and East Central WI with the approach of weak low pressure and a cold front. Several reports of large hail were received, including up to golf ball size.

Bursts of heavy snow showers including a steadier band of snow along a cold front made for hazardous driving conditions. The 1-3 inches of snow and brisk northerly winds reduced visibilities to below 1 mile at times along with snow covered roads. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred across Southeast WI and portions of South Central WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bursts of heavy snow showers with 1-4 inches of snow accumulation. Gusty north winds and reduced visibilities with the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked from the central Great Plains across northern IL and Lower MI, bringing snow, freezing rain, and sleet to southern WI. Heavier snow amounts were toward central WI. Northeast wind gusts in the 30 mph range contributed to reduced visibility and drifting snow toward central WI.  Ice accumulations over one tenth of a inch occurred along and north of a line from Madison to Waukesha but diminished toward central WI. There were numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents due to the snow covered or ice covered roads. There were localized power outages from ice covered tree limbs falling on electrical wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 4 to 7 inches with a very light coating of ice accumulation from freezing rain. Northeast wind gusts in the 30 mph range with drifting snow and reduced visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of May 25th across the western and central sections of Wisconsin along a nearly stationary front. These storms produced large hail and some wind damage from Jackson County south into Crawford County. The largest hail reported was half dollar sized near Arkdale (Adams County). Trees were blown down in Readstown and Hillsboro (Vernon County) and near Black River Falls (Jackson County) where one tree landed on a car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees blown down in Readstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of May 25th across the western and central sections of Wisconsin along a nearly stationary front. These storms produced large hail and some wind damage from Jackson County south into Crawford County. The largest hail reported was half dollar sized near Arkdale (Adams County). Trees were blown down in Readstown and Hillsboro (Vernon County) and near Black River Falls (Jackson County) where one tree landed on a car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wheat field sustained damage from the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed across Southeast and East Central WI with the approach of weak low pressure and a cold front. Several reports of large hail were received, including up to golf ball size.

A surge of mild, moist, and unstable air aloft brought scattered thunderstorms to Southern WI. Two homes suffered significant structural damage from fires after being struck by lightning. Small hail was reported throughout the evening with the largest hail stone the size of a nickel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home causing a significant fire. The fire department reported the home as a total loss. Two people escaped the fire unharmed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked from the central Great Plains across northern IL and Lower MI, bringing snow, freezing rain, and sleet to southern WI. Heavier snow amounts were toward central WI. Northeast wind gusts in the 30 mph range contributed to reduced visibility and drifting snow toward central WI.  Ice accumulations over one tenth of a inch occurred along and north of a line from Madison to Waukesha but diminished toward central WI. There were numerous vehicle slide-offs and accidents due to the snow covered or ice covered roads. There were localized power outages from ice covered tree limbs falling on electrical wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow and less than 0.10 inches of ice accumulation during this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late Winter and early Spring storm produced two areas of heavy snowfall across southern Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the afternoon of March 23rd, through early morning of March 24th. ||The first band of heavy snowfall developed in portions of west central Wisconsin near River Falls and Spring Valley. Initially, this band was disorganized but became a solid enhanced band for several hours during the afternoon. Snowfall rates in this band ranged from 1/2 to locally 2 inches per hour. Due to very dry air on the northern edge of this band, snowfall amounts cutoff dramatically from over a foot, to only a few inches within 20 miles. This band quickly dissipated during the evening and ended before midnight. 6 to 8 inches of snowfall was common before it ended during the evening. ||As the northern band began to dissipate, a secondary area of snowfall developed across northern Iowa, and moved into southern Minnesota during the late evening, and early morning hours of Wednesday March, 24th, and also affected portions of west central Wisconsin around Durand and Eau Claire. This secondary band had snowfall rates as high as 1 to 2 inches per hour, and mainly affected Eau Claire County with locally 10 to 12 inches from south of Eau Claire to Augusta. ||The following are some of the heavier snowfall totals from this storm. ||12.50 inches north of Augusta, WI |12.00 inches in Augusta, WI |10.50 inches in Eau Claire, WI |10.30 inches south of Elk Mound, WI |10.00 inches near Prescott, WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained observers reported 8 to 12 inches of snow in a 9 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of May 31st across western Wisconsin. These storms formed along a nearly stationary front with some of them becoming strong enough to drop large hail. The largest hail reported was quarter sized near Melrose (Monroe County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell near Melrose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rolled across western Wisconsin during the evening of May 3rd. A lightning strike from one of these storms struck propane tanks outside a house north of Black River Falls (Jackson County). Gas leaking from these tanks was on fire, which started a fire in the house that the lines went into.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was damaged by a fire started from a lightning strike. Lightning hit propane tanks outside of the house, which started the gas on fire. The house fire started where the gas lines entered the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced occasional wind gusts of 45 to 60 mph over parts of western and southwest Wisconsin during the morning hours of May 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage occurred at a farm between Ontario and Hillsboro breaking large branches off trees and flipping trailers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 5th across north central Wisconsin ahead of an approaching trough of low pressure. Some of these storms produced wind damage across eastern Taylor County with trees blown down in Rib Lake, Westboro and Medford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Westboro.

Trees were blown down in Readstown and Hillsboro (Vernon County) and near Black River Falls (Jackson County) where one tree landed on a car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on the west side of Black River Falls. One of these trees landed on a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bursts of heavy snow showers including a steadier band of snow along a cold front made for hazardous driving conditions. The 1-3 inches of snow and brisk northerly winds reduced visibilities to below 1 mile at times along with snow covered roads. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred across Southeast WI and portions of South Central WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bursts of heavy snow showers with 1-3 inches of snow accumulation. Gusty north winds and reduced visibilities with the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bursts of accumulating snow occurred as intensifying low pressure moved across Southern WI and into Lower MI. There was an initial burst of snow in the morning followed by numerous heavy snow showers through the day. The snow and wind gusts of 35-50 mph made for snow covered roads and brief blizzard conditions. Numerous slide-offs and accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snow showers producing brief whiteout conditions and snow covered roads. Northwesterly wind gusts of 35-50 mph. Snow accumulation of 1-3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Western Wisconsin experienced strong winds in early April after a cold front moved across the region. Gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common, although a peak gust of 53 mph occurred in La Crosse.  A camper was tipped over by the strong winds on U.S. Highway 18 near Patch Grove (Grant County) and was destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common across La Crosse County during the morning and early afternoon of April 2nd. The highest recorded wind gust was 53 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the La Crosse airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms north of a stationary front produced isolated pea to marble size hail during the afternoon of April 25th.  One of the storms that developed along the front dropped penny size hail as it passed through Newton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Newton observed penny size hail with a passing thunderstorm.  The time of the report was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Parts of western Wisconsin sustained wind damage as thunderstorms moved across the area on the last day of the month. The storms developed ahead of a fast moving cold front and produced sporadic wind damage from the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) southeast to Viroqua (Vernon County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and branches were blown northeast of Stoddard. Around an inch of rain fell is less than an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of May 6th a line of thunderstorms, ahead of an approaching cold front, moved into north central Wisconsin.  One of the storms produced a measured wind gust to 68 mph as it moved over the Manitowish Waters Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees as the storm passed through Manitowish Waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Parts of western Wisconsin sustained wind damage as thunderstorms moved across the area on the last day of the month. The storms developed ahead of a fast moving cold front and produced sporadic wind damage from the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) southeast to Viroqua (Vernon County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Main Street and Bliss Road in the city of La Crosse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms, in the vicinity of a weak stationary front, developed in north central Wisconsin and moved along the Wisconsin/Michigan border.  The storms downed trees and power lines as they passed.  Trees on power lines started fires at many locations in Florence County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines as the storms passed through the Winchester area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that produced large hail and wind damage in southwest Wisconsin moved northeast and merged with another storm that developed in the vicinity of a stationary front.  The storm produced heavy rain, isolated wind damage, and large hail as it moved across Waushara County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree and several 2 to 3 inch diameter branches near Coloma on County Road FF.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that produced large hail and wind damage in southwest Wisconsin moved northeast and merged with another storm that developed in the vicinity of a stationary front.  The storm produced heavy rain, isolated wind damage, and large hail as it moved across Waushara County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were knocked down by thunderstorm winds estimated near 65 mph northwest of Wautoma.  The storm also dropped pea size hail as it passed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed north of a warm front during the afternoon of June 14th. As these storms moved across southwest Wisconsin, they produced heavy rain that caused some flash flooding to occur in and near Prairie du Chien (Crawford County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused street flooding to occur in the city of La Crosse. Some cars became stranded in the flooded streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms approaching from the northwest began to produce damaging winds in central Wisconsin as they encountered a more unstable air mass.  The storms continued to cause damage as they moved across east central Wisconsin. There was also some nickel size hail with the storms. Thunderstorm winds downed or snapped numerous trees, large branches, and power lines; and wrapped a trampoline around a power pole. The winds and/or fallen trees caused roof damage to a middle school, a house, and a garage. The fallen trees and branches caused severe damage to 3 vehicles. Measured gusts from the storms included 60 mph at Manitowoc County Airport (Manitowoc Co.), 62 mph at Appleton International Airport (Outagamie Co.), and 64 mph at Wausau Downtown Airport (Marathon Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked out power to the south side of Brillion.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped nickel size hail as it moved through Milladore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in northwest flow produced isolated thunderstorms. A couple of the storms dropped penny to quarter size hail. Hail covered the ground, causing crop damage in West Bloomfield.  A video recording showed a gustnado, that lasted for about 30 seconds, near Lake Winnebago.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped penny size hail as it passed north of Winnebago.  The time of this event is estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms, a few with large hail, gusty winds, and heavy rain, moved through the area as a low pressure system slid southeast across Wisconsin. Golf ball size hail fell near Readfield (Waupaca Co.) and southwest of Coloma (Waushara Co.). A weak waterspout formed on Lake Winnebago west of Calumet County, but dissipated before reaching shore. A COOP observer near Rhinelander (Oneida Co.) measured 3.04 inches of rain from the storms, much of which fell in about an hour, and a COOP observer in Appleton (Outagamie Co.) had a one-hour rainfall total of 1.66 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak waterspout formed northwest of Stockbridge on eastern Lake Winnebago and moved southeast before dissipating. No damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms, from western Upper Michigan into southeast Minnesota, produced widespread damaging winds that downed a large number of trees and power lines as the storms moved into very warm and unstable air.  A wind gust to 61 mph was measured at Marshfield Municipal Airport (Wood Co.) as the storms passed and a COOP observer in Plover (Portage Co.) measured a gust to 59 mph.  Isolated quarter size hail fell at Hogarty (Marathon Co.) and southwest of Shawano (Shawano Co.), and a brief, weak tornado occurred near Mackville (Outagamie Co.) as the storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed large trees in the Minocqua area. Some of the trees landed on buildings. The time of this event was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought damaging winds, hail, very heavy rain, and a brief tornado, as they moved across central and east central Wisconsin starting in the late afternoon of June 25th, and continuing into the early morning hours of June 26th. The storms downed numerous trees and power lines at many locations as they passed. Thunderstorm winds also tore the roof from a silo and caused shingle and siding damage to a house and garage north of Black Creek (Outagamie Co.), knocked trees onto a house and a car near Seymour (Outagamie Co.), and produced a downburst wind of at least 75 mph that snapped and uprooted trees south of Nichols (Outagamie Co.).||A weak tornado that touched down east-southeast of Symco (Waupaca Co.) snapped and uprooted trees, and damaged several buildings at a farm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell as thunderstorms passed south of Black Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado formed 4.2 miles east-southeast of Symco and traveled east just under three miles. At least a half dozen pine trees were snapped along the path and several large trees were uprooted (DI 28, DOD 3,4). Several farm outbuildings were heavily damaged, with metal from the buildings strewn downwind hundreds of yards. A manufactured home was pushed off its block foundation (DI 3, DOD 3). The tornado dissipated about 6.3 miles north-northeast of Northport. Average path width was 75 yards.

Thunderstorm winds downed or snapped numerous trees, large branches, and power lines; and wrapped a trampoline around a power pole. The winds and/or fallen trees caused roof damage to a middle school, a house, and a garage. The fallen trees and branches caused severe damage to 3 vehicles. Measured gusts from the storms included 60 mph at Manitowoc County Airport (Manitowoc Co.), 62 mph at Appleton International Airport (Outagamie Co.), and 64 mph at Wausau Downtown Airport (Marathon Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds tore part of the roof from a garage near Navarino.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in northwest flow produced isolated thunderstorms. A couple of the storms dropped penny to quarter size hail. Hail covered the ground, causing crop damage in West Bloomfield.  A video recording showed a gustnado, that lasted for about 30 seconds, near Lake Winnebago.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail, up to quarter size, covered the ground and caused some damage to corn crops in West Bloomfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms, a few with large hail, gusty winds, and heavy rain, moved through the area as a low pressure system slid southeast across Wisconsin. Golf ball size hail fell near Readfield (Waupaca Co.) and southwest of Coloma (Waushara Co.). A weak waterspout formed on Lake Winnebago west of Calumet County, but dissipated before reaching shore. A COOP observer near Rhinelander (Oneida Co.) measured 3.04 inches of rain from the storms, much of which fell in about an hour, and a COOP observer in Appleton (Outagamie Co.) had a one-hour rainfall total of 1.66 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a couple of trees in the Gagen area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and a cold front produced isolated thunderstorms, a couple of which became severe, across central Wisconsin. One of the storms dropped quarter size hail near Marathon City (Marathon Co.) and another produced a brief tornado near Poniatowski (Marathon Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Marathon City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and a cold front produced isolated thunderstorms, a couple of which became severe, across central Wisconsin. One of the storms dropped quarter size hail near Marathon City (Marathon Co.) and another produced a brief tornado near Poniatowski (Marathon Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado produced minor damage at a farm near Poniatowski. An old barn was pushed over by winds estimated at 60 mph. The tornado was rated EF0 and was on the ground for less than a tenth of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed north through central Wisconsin ahead of another frontal system over Minnesota/Iowa.  Isolated storm mergers along or near the warm front produced: winds that downed trees, branches and power lines; hail to nickel size; and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed at least two trees and several branches about two miles north of Highway 10, on Shady Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very weak tornado (waterspout) occurred on Loon Lake in northern Shawano County as a line of showers interacted with a surface boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very weak waterspout occurred on Loon Lake, about five miles northeast of the city of Shawano. The waterspout, which was photographed and recorded by law enforcement, remained nearly stationary for about a minute, causing no damage, before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought damaging winds, hail, very heavy rain, and a brief tornado, as they moved across central and east central Wisconsin starting in the late afternoon of June 25th, and continuing into the early morning hours of June 26th. The storms downed numerous trees and power lines at many locations as they passed. Thunderstorm winds also tore the roof from a silo and caused shingle and siding damage to a house and garage north of Black Creek (Outagamie Co.), knocked trees onto a house and a car near Seymour (Outagamie Co.), and produced a downburst wind of at least 75 mph that snapped and uprooted trees south of Nichols (Outagamie Co.).||A weak tornado that touched down east-southeast of Symco (Waupaca Co.) snapped and uprooted trees, and damaged several buildings at a farm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and knocked out power in Presque Isle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: About a dozen trees were snapped or damaged and several were uprooted south of Nichols due to thunderstorm downburst winds that were estimated at least 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser observed a funnel cloud for several minutes near the town of Martell, or west of Spring Valley in west central Wisconsin. No damage was reported but the funnel was only a few hundred feet above the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser recorded a funnel cloud for several minutes east of Martell. It was evident that the funnel cloud was only a few hundred feet above the ground. No damage was observed or reported, nor was there any indication of a touchdown on video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from previous morning thunderstorms across central Minnesota Friday morning, June 10th, ignited a broken line of severe thunderstorms in northwest Wisconsin Friday afternoon. These storms sagged southward into west central Wisconsin where several thunderstorms developed near Rice Lake and moved eastward across Rusk County. ||More thunderstorms quickly developed in southeast Minnesota and west central Wisconsin, from Red Wing to Dunn County near Menomonie. These storms quickly became severe which blew down numerous trees and power lines, and also affected the Blue Ox Festival where trees fell on cars and camper trailers in Eau Claire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were toppled, and a barn door was ripped off. It occurred approximately 14 miles east of Ellsworth near County Road 72.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms, from western Upper Michigan into southeast Minnesota, produced widespread damaging winds that downed a large number of trees and power lines as the storms moved into very warm and unstable air.  A wind gust to 61 mph was measured at Marshfield Municipal Airport (Wood Co.) as the storms passed and a COOP observer in Plover (Portage Co.) measured a gust to 59 mph.  Isolated quarter size hail fell at Hogarty (Marathon Co.) and southwest of Shawano (Shawano Co.), and a brief, weak tornado occurred near Mackville (Outagamie Co.) as the storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado (EF0) developed about 1.3 miles southwest of Mackville and moved southeast before dissipating. Damage was limited to broken tree branches and limbs. Peak wind was estimated at 55 to 60 mph. The tornado was photographed by a storm spotter.

These storms sagged southward into west central Wisconsin where several thunderstorms developed near Rice Lake and moved eastward across Rusk County. ||More thunderstorms quickly developed in southeast Minnesota and west central Wisconsin, from Red Wing to Dunn County near Menomonie. These storms quickly became severe which blew down numerous trees and power lines, and also affected the Blue Ox Festival where trees fell on cars and camper trailers in Eau Claire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down along Highway I, near Barry Corner and Devils Corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms approaching from the northwest began to produce damaging winds in central Wisconsin as they encountered a more unstable air mass.  The storms continued to cause damage as they moved across east central Wisconsin. There was also some nickel size hail with the storms. Thunderstorm winds downed or snapped numerous trees, large branches, and power lines; and wrapped a trampoline around a power pole. The winds and/or fallen trees caused roof damage to a middle school, a house, and a garage. The fallen trees and branches caused severe damage to 3 vehicles. Measured gusts from the storms included 60 mph at Manitowoc County Airport (Manitowoc Co.), 62 mph at Appleton International Airport (Outagamie Co.), and 64 mph at Wausau Downtown Airport (Marathon Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped 5 large pine trees in New London.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms, from western Upper Michigan into southeast Minnesota, produced widespread damaging winds that downed a large number of trees and power lines as the storms moved into very warm and unstable air.  A wind gust to 61 mph was measured at Marshfield Municipal Airport (Wood Co.) as the storms passed and a COOP observer in Plover (Portage Co.) measured a gust to 59 mph.  Isolated quarter size hail fell at Hogarty (Marathon Co.) and southwest of Shawano (Shawano Co.), and a brief, weak tornado occurred near Mackville (Outagamie Co.) as the storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds downed trees on Highway 47 around Keshena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that bowed out across central and northern Minnesota, moved east-southeast across northwest and west central Wisconsin during the morning of Sunday, July 21st. An outflow boundary was generated with the intense thunderstorms and produced numerous uprooted and blown down trees across west central Wisconsin. The most concentrated area of damage occurred around Rice Lake where structural damage occurred to trailers.||As the storms continued to move eastward, sporadic tree damage occurred across mainly northern Chippewa County, where the local sheriff's office recorded 22 individual tree damage calls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down west of Haugen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Parts of western Wisconsin sustained wind damage as thunderstorms moved across the area on the last day of the month. The storms developed ahead of a fast moving cold front and produced sporadic wind damage from the city of La Crosse (La Crosse County) southeast to Viroqua (Vernon County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Viroqua. The automated weather observing equipment at the airport on the north side of town, measured a wind gust of 54 mph. However, radar data indicates a small corridor of stronger winds went across the western sections of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that bowed out across central and northern Minnesota, moved east-southeast across northwest and west central Wisconsin during the morning of Sunday, July 21st. An outflow boundary was generated with the intense thunderstorms and produced numerous uprooted and blown down trees across west central Wisconsin. The most concentrated area of damage occurred around Rice Lake where structural damage occurred to trailers.||As the storms continued to move eastward, sporadic tree damage occurred across mainly northern Chippewa County, where the local sheriff's office recorded 22 individual tree damage calls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees, up to 2.5 feet in diameter, were blown down or uprooted from the north side of Rice Lake, to the east side of the city limits. The most concentrated area was north and east along County Road 48.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Tuesday, July 5th, two areas of thunderstorms that developed across west central Minnesota, moved southeast across central and southern Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin.  ||The southern storm affected the southern part of the Twin Cities metro area during the late afternoon, and moved eastward into west central Wisconsin from near Prescott and Hager City to Eau Claire Wisconsin by early evening. These storms produced damaging winds and two tornadoes near Plum City and Porcupine.||The hardest hit areas were in Pierce County from Prescott and Bay City, eastward to Ellsworth, Salem and Maiden Rock. Hundreds of trees were blown down with some structural damage to bridges near Ellsworth and home damage in Plum City. Local law enforcement officers reported a swept away trailer house at Highway 10 and 490th Avenue in the Town of Salem. There was also damage to a milking parlor in Ellsworth where 3 feet of water filled the calf barn. ||The Pepin County Emergency Manager released a press statement that provided detailed information on widespread damage throughout the county. There was also structural damage to homes and agricultural, and at least three commercial properties in the Town of Waubeek's business park were damaged. ||In addition to structural damage to homes and businesses, crop damage was extensive and the local emergency management submitted a disaster report based|on the widespread nature of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesocyclone originated in Pierce County and caused widespread downburst damage across a 1.5 mile wide area.  It knocked down many hundreds of trees, and partially removed the roof off of one house.  The mesocyclone continued into Pepin County, where it continued to produce wind damage and then a tornado (see separate entries for Pepin County).

Very heavy rain resulted in flash flooding, some street flooding; in addition to some hail with the storms. The roof of a home in Oconomowoc was struck by lightning with a resultant fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of May 25th across the western and central sections of Wisconsin along a nearly stationary front. These storms produced large hail and some wind damage from Jackson County south into Crawford County. The largest hail reported was half dollar sized near Arkdale (Adams County). Trees were blown down in Readstown and Hillsboro (Vernon County) and near Black River Falls (Jackson County) where one tree landed on a car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar sized hail fell in Mauston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 3rd across parts of western Wisconsin ahead of an approaching warm front. Some of these storms became strong enough to produce sporadic wind damage across portions of Trempealeau, Jackson and Clark Counties. The most significant damage occurred in Independence (Trempealeau County) where multiple trees and a bank sign were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Northfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down south of Osseo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of warm and moist air on a low level jet stream resulted in numerous thunderstorms over southern Wisconsin. Very heavy rain resulted in flash flooding, some street flooding; in addition to some hail with the storms. The roof of a home in Oconomowoc was struck by lightning with a resultant fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6.5 inches of rain in two hours brought flash flooding of creeks and fields in the Town of Sumner. Many roads were washed out including state highway 106 from Schwemmer Road to Kreutz Road with a foot drop off in some areas. The Becker Road bridge sustained excessive damage on the sidewalls due to large tree limbs blocking the middle of the bridge, thereby diverting water around the sides. Up to 2 feet of water was observed going over the bridge. Approximately 100 or more acres of farmland was flooded mainly near North Shore Road, High Ridge Road, and Burnham Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees and tree branches down on Lamp Road and Blackhawk Drive. A tree fell on a car.

Trees were blown down in Readstown and Hillsboro (Vernon County) and near Black River Falls (Jackson County) where one tree landed on a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that bowed out across central and northern Minnesota, moved east-southeast across northwest and west central Wisconsin during the morning of Sunday, July 21st. An outflow boundary was generated with the intense thunderstorms and produced numerous uprooted and blown down trees across west central Wisconsin. The most concentrated area of damage occurred around Rice Lake where structural damage occurred to trailers.||As the storms continued to move eastward, sporadic tree damage occurred across mainly northern Chippewa County, where the local sheriff's office recorded 22 individual tree damage calls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic tree damage occurred across the county. However, the most concentrated area was at Clear Lake County Park, where a local microburst caused considerable damage. Numerous trees were blown down in the park and campground, which is northwest of the town of Clear Lake. The park had to close temporarily due to widespread damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that bowed out across central and northern Minnesota, moved east-southeast across northwest and west central Wisconsin during the morning of Sunday, July 21st. An outflow boundary was generated with the intense thunderstorms and produced numerous uprooted and blown down trees across west central Wisconsin. The most concentrated area of damage occurred around Rice Lake where structural damage occurred to trailers.||As the storms continued to move eastward, sporadic tree damage occurred across mainly northern Chippewa County, where the local sheriff's office recorded 22 individual tree damage calls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic tree damage occurred across the county but the majority of the damage was across northern Chippewa County, between Highway 40 and Lake Holcombe. The local dispatch at the sheriff's office recorded 22 individual reports of downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Tuesday, July 5th, two areas of thunderstorms that developed across west central Minnesota, moved southeast across central and southern Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin.  ||The southern storm affected the southern part of the Twin Cities metro area during the late afternoon, and moved eastward into west central Wisconsin from near Prescott and Hager City to Eau Claire Wisconsin by early evening. These storms produced damaging winds and two tornadoes near Plum City and Porcupine.||The hardest hit areas were in Pierce County from Prescott and Bay City, eastward to Ellsworth, Salem and Maiden Rock. Hundreds of trees were blown down with some structural damage to bridges near Ellsworth and home damage in Plum City. Local law enforcement officers reported a swept away trailer house at Highway 10 and 490th Avenue in the Town of Salem. There was also damage to a milking parlor in Ellsworth where 3 feet of water filled the calf barn. ||The Pepin County Emergency Manager released a press statement that provided detailed information on widespread damage throughout the county. There was also structural damage to homes and agricultural, and at least three commercial properties in the Town of Waubeek's business park were damaged. ||In addition to structural damage to homes and businesses, crop damage was extensive and the local emergency management submitted a disaster report based|on the widespread nature of the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down on the north end of Nugget Lake, knocking down trees. There was also downburst damage near the tornado (see separate entry for the wind damage).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and a bank sign were blown down in Independence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was embedded within a broader mesocyclone that was sometimes three miles wide (see separate entry for the wind damage from the mesocyclone). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and warm front resulted in numerous thunderstorms over southern WI during the afternoon and early evening. One EF-1 tornado occurred in Fond du lac County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado damaged the roofs of approximately 10 homes, 2 businesses, and 1 church. The roof damage covered less than 20 percent of the roof in most cases. The damage ranged from the removal of shingles to the entire removal of the roof material. Awnings and gutters were also damaged on the structures including a small number of windows.  Numerous tree limbs were downed including some trees snapped at the base of the trunk, and approximately one tree uprooted. One large 20 foot trailer was overturned by the tornadic winds.

Thunderstorms that moved across western Wisconsin early Wednesday morning, July 13th, produced a batch of large hail from Glenwood City to Wheeler. Most of the hail was small, but a few were as large as quarters.

A surge of warm and moist air on a low level jet stream brought a large thunderstorm complex across southern WI from the evening into the early morning hours. Heavy rain and isolated flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road KP was closed due to flooding between South Valley Road and Scherbel Road, which is between Black Earth and Cross Plains. 1.5 feet of water on the roads in the Town of Berry near highway 19 and County Road KP. Shoulder washouts on highway 19 east to Indian Lake. Shoulder washouts in various locations on County Road KP between highway 14 and highway 19. Shoulder washouts also on highway V from Loeper Road and to the west on V. Doppler radar estimates 5 to 8 inches of rain in a 6 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of warm and moist air on a low level jet stream brought a large thunderstorm complex across southern WI from the evening into the early morning hours. Heavy rain and isolated flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway F was flooded and closed between highway FF and Pleasant Valley Road due to the flash flooding of the east branch of Blue Mounds Creek.  There were also shoulder washouts on highway F between Moyer Road and Zwettler Road. There were also shoulder washouts on highway JG between Little Norway Road and North Road.   Approximately 5 to 7 inches of rain had fallen in about 5 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a line of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the afternoon of September 19th, some of the stronger storms dropped large hail. Quarter sized hail was reported in Bloom City (Richland County), near Rockbridge (Richland County) and in Glen Haven (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in Bloom City.
